questions.text: When does a React component re-render? List all triggers.
questions.good: Mentions state changes, prop changes, and parent re-renders. Understands the basic concept.
questions.great: Also mentions context changes, force updates, and incorrectly mutating state. Mentions React 18's automatic batching.
questions.red_flag: 'When I click a button.' or 'When the page loads.' Shows no understanding of the rendering lifecycle.
questions.follow_up: What happens if you mutate state directly instead of using setState?

questions.text: Explain the difference between useEffect and useLayoutEffect. When would you use each?
questions.good: Knows useEffect runs after paint, useLayoutEffect runs before paint. Uses useLayoutEffect for DOM measurements.
questions.great: Discusses performance implications, mentions useInsertionEffect (React 18), and gives specific examples (animations, scroll position, avoiding visual flicker).
questions.red_flag: 'They're the same thing.' or 'I always use useEffect.' Doesn't understand the visual/performance distinction.
questions.follow_up: Have you ever had a bug where switching from useEffect to useLayoutEffect fixed it?

questions.text: When should you use useMemo and useCallback? Give a specific example where NOT using them causes a real problem.
questions.good: Understands they memoize values and functions. Mentions avoiding unnecessary re-renders of child components.
questions.great: Discusses the cost of memoization vs. the cost of re-rendering. Mentions dependency array correctness. Gives a concrete example. Mentions React Compiler (React 19).
questions.red_flag: 'I use them everywhere to make my app faster.' (Premature optimization.) Or 'I don't know what those are.'
questions.follow_up: What happens if you get the dependency array wrong?

questions.text: What are React Server Components (RSC), and how do they change the way you build React apps?
questions.good: Knows RSC runs on the server, reduces bundle size, and can access server resources directly. Mentions Next.js App Router.
questions.great: Explains the mental model shift: Server vs. Client boundaries, use client directive, when to use RSC vs. Client Components, data fetching patterns, and the 'zero JS' bundle benefit. Discusses trade-offs.
questions.red_flag: 'It's just server-side rendering.' or 'I haven't used it yet.' (For a senior role, this is concerning.)
questions.follow_up: In a Next.js App Router project, how do you decide whether a component should be a Server Component or a Client Component?

questions.text: Describe a complex state management scenario you've handled. How did you structure it?
questions.good: Describes a multi-component state problem. Used Context API, Redux, or Zustand. Explains the choice.
questions.great: Discusses state normalization, derived state, when to lift state vs. keep local. Mentions specific libraries (Zustand, Jotai, Redux Toolkit) and why they chose one. Talks about performance.
questions.red_flag: 'I put everything in Redux.' or 'I use useState for everything.' (No architectural thinking.)
questions.follow_up: What would you do differently if you had to scale that state to 50 components?

questions.text: How do you handle composition in React? What's the difference between children and render props?
questions.good: Understands children as a prop for nested content. Knows render props pattern for inversion of control.
questions.great: Discusses compound components, slots, and when to use each pattern. Mentions modern alternatives (React Context + hooks, headless UI patterns). Understands composition over inheritance.
questions.red_flag: 'I don't use composition.' or only understands children as a basic prop.
questions.follow_up: When would you choose a render prop over a compound component?

questions.text: Explain generics in TypeScript. Give a real example where you used them to make your code more reusable.
questions.good: Defines generics as type variables. Gives an example (e.g., a typed API client, a generic component).
questions.great: Discusses generic constraints (extends), default types, mapped types, and conditional types. Gives a production example (e.g., type-safe form builder, generic table component).
questions.red_flag: 'I don't use generics.' or 'Generics are too complicated.' (TypeScript without generics is just JavaScript with extra steps.)
questions.follow_up: What's the difference between T extends string and T = string?

questions.text: How do you ensure type safety across the frontend-backend boundary? Describe your approach to API contracts.
questions.good: Mentions shared types, OpenAPI/Swagger, or GraphQL schemas. Uses TypeScript interfaces for API responses.
questions.great: Describes a full-stack type system: auto-generated types from backend (tRPC, GraphQL codegen, OpenAPI generator), runtime validation (Zod, Valibot), and end-to-end type safety.
questions.red_flag: 'I just use any for API responses.' or 'I trust the backend to send the right data.'
questions.follow_up: What happens when the backend changes a field name and you have any everywhere?

questions.text: What are utility types in TypeScript, and which ones do you use most often?
questions.good: Mentions Partial, Required, Pick, Omit, Record. Knows what they do.
questions.great: Discusses advanced utility types: ReturnType, Parameters, Awaited, NonNullable. Explains when to use each. Mentions custom utility types they've built.
questions.red_flag: Only knows Partial and Pick. Or 'I don't use utility types.'
questions.follow_up: How would you create a type that makes all properties of an interface optional except one?

questions.text: How would you integrate a Large Language Model (LLM) into a React application? Walk me through the architecture.
questions.good: Mentions API calls to OpenAI/Anthropic. Uses fetch or a library. Handles loading states and errors.
questions.great: Discusses streaming responses (SSE/WebSocket), state management for conversation history, error handling, optimistic UI, and the Vercel AI SDK. Mentions client-side vs. server-side AI architecture.
questions.red_flag: 'I would just call the API directly from the frontend.' (No architecture thinking, no security awareness.)
questions.follow_up: What are the security risks of calling the LLM API directly from the frontend?

questions.text: Your AI feature processes 10,000 user requests/day. Each request sends a 2,000-token prompt to GPT-4. Your monthly budget is $500. How do you stay within budget?
questions.good: Mentions caching, prompt optimization, using cheaper models, and rate limiting.
questions.great: Calculates exact costs: 10,000 × 2,000 tokens × $0.03/1K tokens ≈ $600/day. Explains the budget is impossible without optimization. Proposes: reducing prompt size, using GPT-3.5 for 80% of requests, caching, hybrid approach.
questions.red_flag: 'I'll just use the cheapest model.' (No understanding of token economics, quality trade-offs, or optimization strategies.)
questions.follow_up: What is a token, and how do you count them?

questions.text: What is a 'context window,' and why does it matter for AI applications? How do you handle conversations that exceed it?
questions.good: Explains the context window as the maximum tokens a model can process. Mentions truncation as a risk.
questions.great: Discusses different models' context limits (4K, 8K, 128K, 2M). Explains the trade-off between context length and cost. Describes strategies: chunking, summarization, RAG, sliding window.
questions.red_flag: 'It's how long the AI can talk.' or 'I just send the whole conversation.' (No technical understanding of the constraint.)
questions.follow_up: If a user has a 50-message chat history, how do you decide which messages to include in the next prompt?

questions.text: Walk me through building a RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) system from scratch. What are the key components?
questions.good: Mentions: document ingestion → chunking → embedding → vector database → retrieval → context injection → generation.
questions.great: Discusses chunking strategies (fixed-size, semantic, recursive), embedding models, vector databases, retrieval strategies (similarity search, hybrid search, re-ranking), context window management, and evaluation metrics.
questions.red_flag: 'You just upload documents to ChatGPT.' or 'RAG is just giving the AI a PDF.' (Completely misunderstands RAG architecture.)
questions.follow_up: What are the limitations of RAG, and when would you NOT use it?

questions.text: How do you use AI-powered IDEs (Cursor, Windsurf, GitHub Copilot) differently from a regular IDE like VS Code?
questions.good: Mentions AI-powered autocomplete, inline editing, and chat interface.
questions.great: Discusses the paradigm shift: context-aware generation (whole files, not just lines), AI-driven refactoring, test generation, documentation generation, custom rules (.cursorrules), composer mode for multi-file changes.
questions.red_flag: 'It's just VS Code with AI.' or 'I don't use AI tools.' (Misses the productivity revolution or is resistant to it.)
questions.follow_up: Describe a custom AI workflow you've built that improved your productivity.

questions.text: What are design tokens, and why are they important? How would you set them up for a product with multiple themes?
questions.good: Defines tokens as variables for colors, spacing, typography. Mentions consistency and theming.
questions.great: Discusses token hierarchy (primitive → semantic → component), theming (dark mode, brand variants), cross-platform usage (CSS variables, Figma variables, Tailwind config), and developer handoff automation.
questions.red_flag: 'I don't know what that means.' or 'I just use hex codes.' (No design system awareness.)
questions.follow_up: What's the difference between a primitive token and a semantic token?

questions.text: How do you create a component library from scratch? What makes a component 'good'?
questions.good: Mentions reusability, variants, states, and documentation. Uses a library like Storybook.
questions.great: Discusses accessibility (ARIA, keyboard navigation), API design (props, composition), testing (unit, visual regression), versioning (changesets), and the boundary between system components and product components.
questions.red_flag: 'I just copy-paste from my old designs.' or 'Component libraries are overkill.'
questions.follow_up: What do you do when a component needs to be slightly different in two different places?

questions.text: How do you implement dark mode in a React application? What are the challenges?
questions.good: Uses CSS variables or a theme provider. Toggles a class or data attribute.
questions.great: Discusses system preference detection (prefers-color-scheme), persistent user preference, smooth transitions (no flash), image handling, and the CSS variable architecture. Mentions tools like next-themes or Tailwind's dark mode.
questions.red_flag: 'I just swap the CSS file.' or 'I haven't implemented dark mode.' (For a modern developer, this is a basic requirement.)
questions.follow_up: How do you prevent a flash of the wrong theme on page load?

questions.text: How do you design a REST API endpoint for a complex feature? What considerations go into the design?
questions.good: Mentions HTTP methods, status codes, request/response structure, and validation.
questions.great: Discusses pagination, filtering, sorting, rate limiting, idempotency, versioning, error handling, and caching. Mentions OpenAPI specs and type-safe clients.
questions.red_flag: 'I just make endpoints that return JSON.' or 'I let the backend team decide.' (No API design thinking.)
questions.follow_up: How do you handle an API endpoint that returns 10,000 records?

questions.text: What database would you choose for a real-time chat application, and why?
questions.good: Mentions PostgreSQL, MongoDB, or Firebase. Gives a reason (relations, scalability, real-time).
questions.great: Discusses the full stack: PostgreSQL for persistence, Redis for pub/sub, WebSocket for real-time, and message queues for reliability. Considers trade-offs: consistency vs. availability, horizontal scaling.
questions.red_flag: 'I always use MongoDB because it's easy.' or 'I don't know, I just use whatever the backend team gives me.'
questions.follow_up: How do you handle messages that fail to deliver?

questions.text: A React app you built is running slowly. Walk me through your debugging process.
questions.good: Mentions React DevTools Profiler, Chrome DevTools, checking for unnecessary re-renders, and memoization.
questions.great: Describes a systematic process: reproduce → measure (Profiler, Lighthouse) → identify bottleneck (re-renders, bundle size, network) → fix → verify. Mentions specific tools (why-did-you-render, bundle analyzer).
questions.red_flag: 'I just add useMemo everywhere.' or 'I don't know how to debug performance.'
questions.follow_up: What's the most impactful performance fix you've made?

questions.text: You're asked to add a feature that will take 2 weeks, but the founder wants it in 3 days. How do you handle this?
questions.good: Pushes back respectfully, explains the trade-offs, proposes a phased approach (MVP first).
questions.great: Frames the conversation around business value and risk. Proposes a 'time-boxed' MVP that delivers 80% of the value in 3 days. Discusses technical debt and documents the compromise.
questions.red_flag: 'I'll just work harder.' or 'I can't do it, sorry.' (Either burns out or gives up without trying to find a solution.)
questions.follow_up: What would you say if the founder insists on the full feature in 3 days?

questions.text: What do you want to learn in the next year? How does that align with Digidelsolutions's direction?
questions.good: Specific skill or area (e.g., 'AI architecture', 'design systems'). Shows some awareness of the company.
questions.great: Connects personal growth to company goals. Has a self-directed learning plan. Shows curiosity about AI and its application to real products.
questions.red_flag: 'I don't know.' or 'Nothing, I'm already good.' or a generic answer unrelated to the company.
questions.follow_up: How do you plan to learn that?

questions.text: Describe a time you owned a project end-to-end. What was the hardest part, and how did you handle it?
questions.good: Describes a project they led from start to finish. Mentions a specific challenge and how they overcame it.
questions.great: Shows ownership mentality: they identified the problem, proposed the solution, got buy-in, executed, and measured results. The 'hardest part' was non-technical.
questions.red_flag: 'I haven't owned a project end-to-end.' or 'The hardest part was the coding.' (Shows no ownership or growth mindset.)
questions.follow_up: What would you do differently if you had to do it again?

questions.text: Explain the difference between UI and UX. Give an example where great UI failed because of bad UX.
questions.good: Clear distinction: UI is visual, UX is the overall experience. Example is relevant (e.g., a beautiful app with confusing navigation).
questions.great: References specific case studies (e.g., Snapchat's 2018 redesign, Windows 8). Discusses business impact (drop in user engagement, increase in support tickets).
questions.red_flag: Thinks UI and UX are the same thing. Cannot give a real example. Says 'UI is how it looks, UX is how it works' without elaboration.
questions.follow_up: How would you fix the bad UX in your example?

questions.text: How do you approach accessibility in your designs? What standards do you follow?
questions.good: Mentions WCAG, color contrast (4.5:1), font sizes, screen reader awareness, keyboard navigation.
questions.great: Discusses inclusive design, testing with real users, accessibility-first workflow, focus management, and semantic HTML. Mentions tools like axe, WAVE, or Lighthouse.
questions.red_flag: 'I don't think about it much.' or 'My designs look good, so it's accessible.'
questions.follow_up: What contrast ratio do you aim for body text? What about large text?

questions.text: Walk me through your design process for a new feature from start to finish.
questions.good: Mentions research → wireframes → mockups → feedback → handoff. Shows a linear process.
questions.great: Includes user research (interviews, surveys, analytics), data analysis, competitive analysis, A/B testing, developer collaboration, and post-launch metrics. Mentions iterative design and when to pivot.
questions.red_flag: Jumps straight to 'I open Figma and start designing.' No research phase. No testing.
questions.follow_up: How do you decide which design to test first?

questions.text: How do you use hierarchy, color, and typography to guide user attention?
questions.good: Mentions size, contrast, white space, and font weight. Understands visual hierarchy basics.
questions.great: Discusses Gestalt principles (proximity, similarity, continuity), F-pattern and Z-pattern reading, color psychology, and typographic scale (modular scale). Gives specific examples of how they used these to solve a real problem.
questions.red_flag: 'I just make it look good.' or 'I follow trends.' (No design theory foundation.)
questions.follow_up: Show me a design where you intentionally broke a hierarchy rule — why?

questions.text: What is your approach to mobile-responsive design? How do you ensure consistency across devices?
questions.good: Mentions breakpoints, flexible grids, and media queries. Uses a mobile-first approach.
questions.great: Discusses content strategy for mobile (what to show/hide), touch targets, performance considerations, and testing on real devices. Mentions design systems that support responsive patterns.
questions.red_flag: 'I just shrink the desktop design.' or 'Responsive design is the developer's job.'
questions.follow_up: How do you handle a navigation menu that has 8 items on desktop but only fits 3 on mobile?

questions.text: What is your Figma workflow? Walk me through how you structure a design file for a complex project.
questions.good: Uses pages for organization, frames for screens, components for reusable elements, and auto-layout.
questions.great: Discusses design systems in Figma (variables, component properties, variants), developer handoff (Dev Mode, annotations), and collaboration (comments, branching). Mentions plugins and automation.
questions.red_flag: 'I just design on one page.' or 'I don't use components.' (No workflow discipline.)
questions.follow_up: How do you handle design handoff to developers?

questions.text: How do you use AI tools (Midjourney, DALL-E, Adobe Firefly) in your design process?
questions.good: Mentions using AI for ideation, mood boards, or generating placeholder images. Understands the basics.
questions.great: Describes a workflow where AI accelerates exploration: generating variations, A/B testing concepts, creating custom illustrations, and iterating faster. Discusses ethical considerations and when NOT to use AI.
questions.red_flag: 'I don't use AI tools.' or 'AI will replace designers, so I avoid it.' (Misses the productivity gain or is resistant to change.)
questions.follow_up: Show me a design where AI helped you explore 10x more concepts than you could manually.

questions.text: Describe a time you used user research to change a design decision. What was the research, and what did you change?
questions.good: Mentions a specific research method (interviews, surveys, usability testing). Describes a change they made based on findings.
questions.great: Shows a full research cycle: hypothesis → method → findings → design change → validation. Discusses sample size, bias, and how they communicated findings to stakeholders.
questions.red_flag: 'I don't do user research.' or 'I just design what I think is best.' (No user-centered thinking.)
questions.follow_up: How did you convince the team to make the change?

questions.text: How do you measure the success of a design after launch? What metrics do you track?
questions.good: Mentions usability metrics (task completion rate, error rate), engagement metrics (time on page, clicks), and qualitative feedback.
questions.great: Connects design metrics to business outcomes (conversion rate, churn, NPS). Describes a measurement framework they use. Discusses how they set baselines and targets.
questions.red_flag: 'I don't measure success.' or 'I just look at whether people like it.' (No data-driven thinking.)
questions.follow_up: What would you do if the metrics show your design performed worse than the old one?

questions.text: How do you build and maintain a design system? What makes a design system successful?
questions.good: Mentions components, styles, documentation, and governance. Uses a tool like Figma or Storybook.
questions.great: Discusses adoption strategies (incentives, not enforcement), versioning, contribution models, and scaling. Mentions token-based architecture and cross-platform consistency. Describes a system they built or maintained.
questions.red_flag: 'Design systems are too restrictive.' or 'I just create components when needed.' (No systematic thinking.)
questions.follow_up: How do you get developers to actually use the design system?

questions.text: How do you handle feedback from stakeholders who don't understand design?
questions.good: Listens actively, asks clarifying questions, and explains design decisions in business terms.
questions.great: Uses framing techniques: 'This design increases conversion by X% because...' or 'User testing showed that...' Builds trust through data and transparency. Knows when to push back and when to compromise.
questions.red_flag: 'I just do what they say.' or 'They don't understand design, so I ignore them.' (Either a doormat or arrogant.)
questions.follow_up: What do you do when a founder insists on a design you know is bad?

questions.text: What design trend or technology are you most excited about right now? Why?
questions.good: Mentions a specific trend (e.g., AI-generated design, spatial design, micro-interactions). Shows genuine interest.
questions.great: Connects the trend to real user problems or business value. Has experimented with it. Shows a learning mindset and curiosity.
questions.red_flag: 'I don't follow trends.' or 'Design trends are just fads.' (Shows no curiosity or adaptability.)
questions.follow_up: How would you experiment with that trend in your next project?

questions.text: Describe a time you had to advocate for a design decision that was unpopular. How did you handle it?
questions.good: Describes a specific situation. Explains how they presented their case and reached a compromise.
questions.great: Shows diplomacy and data-driven advocacy. Used user research, A/B testing, or competitor analysis to support their case. Describes the outcome and what they learned.
questions.red_flag: 'I never advocate for unpopular decisions.' or 'I just do what the team wants.' (No backbone or leadership.)
questions.follow_up: What if the data supported the unpopular decision but the team still rejected it?

questions.text: Write a 50-word social media post about AI for a startup audience. Do it now, out loud.
questions.good: Clear, engaging, appropriate tone, no errors. Conveys the message.
questions.great: Has a hook, includes a CTA, considers platform, uses relevant hashtags. Shows brand awareness.
questions.red_flag: Grammatical errors, boring tone, no understanding of the audience.
questions.follow_up: How would you adapt this for Twitter vs. LinkedIn?

questions.text: How do you adapt your writing tone for different audiences? Give examples.
questions.good: Mentions adjusting vocabulary, sentence length, and formality based on audience. Gives examples (e.g., technical vs. casual).
questions.great: Discusses audience research, personas, and brand voice guidelines. Shows versatility across formats (blog, social, email, whitepaper).
questions.red_flag: 'I write the same way for everyone.' or 'I just write what sounds good to me.' (No audience awareness.)
questions.follow_up: Write the same headline for a technical audience and a non-technical audience.

questions.text: How do you use AI writing tools (ChatGPT, Jasper, Grammarly) in your workflow?
questions.good: Mentions using AI for ideation, drafting, or editing. Understands the tool's limitations.
questions.great: Describes a hybrid workflow: AI for first drafts or research, human for tone, accuracy, and brand alignment. Discusses when AI helps and when it hurts. Shows critical thinking about AI-generated content.
questions.red_flag: 'I don't use AI tools.' or 'I just copy-paste what ChatGPT gives me.' (Either resistant or lazy.)
questions.follow_up: Edit this AI-generated paragraph to make it sound more human and on-brand.

questions.text: How do you approach keyword research for a new blog post? What tools do you use?
questions.good: Mentions keyword research tools (Ahrefs, SEMrush, Google Keyword Planner). Considers search volume and competition.
questions.great: Discusses search intent (informational, transactional, navigational), SERP analysis, long-tail keywords, and content gaps. Describes a systematic process they follow.
questions.red_flag: 'I just write about what I think is interesting.' or 'I don't do keyword research.' (No SEO awareness.)
questions.follow_up: What would you do if your target keyword has a high difficulty score?

questions.text: Describe a content strategy you developed or executed. What was the goal, and how did you measure success?
questions.good: Describes a content initiative with a clear goal. Mentions metrics like traffic, engagement, or leads.
questions.great: Connects content strategy to business outcomes (revenue, brand awareness, customer retention). Describes the full funnel: awareness → consideration → conversion. Shows data-driven thinking.
questions.red_flag: 'I just write articles.' or 'I don't know what content strategy means.' (No strategic thinking.)
questions.follow_up: What would you change if you had to redo that strategy?

questions.text: Edit this paragraph in real time: [Provide a paragraph with grammar errors, awkward phrasing, and inconsistent tone].
questions.good: Catches grammar errors and awkward phrasing. Improves clarity and flow.
questions.great: Fixes grammar, improves tone consistency, strengthens the message, and suggests structural improvements. Explains why they made each change.
questions.red_flag: Misses obvious errors. Makes the text worse. Doesn't understand tone or style.
questions.follow_up: How would you edit this for a C-suite audience vs. a developer audience?

questions.text: How do you ensure factual accuracy in your content, especially when writing about technical topics?
questions.good: Mentions fact-checking, citing sources, and consulting subject matter experts.
questions.great: Describes a verification process: cross-referencing sources, using primary sources, interviewing experts, and maintaining a source library. Discusses how they handle conflicting information.
questions.red_flag: 'I just trust Google.' or 'I don't fact-check because I'm a writer, not a researcher.' (No accountability.)
questions.follow_up: What do you do when a source contradicts your previous article?

questions.text: What content creators or brands do you admire? Why?
questions.good: Mentions specific creators or brands. Explains what they admire (voice, strategy, consistency).
questions.great: Analyzes their content strategy, not just the output. Discusses how they would apply similar techniques to Digidelsolutions. Shows genuine enthusiasm for the craft.
questions.red_flag: 'I don't follow any content creators.' or 'I don't read much.' (No curiosity or learning mindset.)
questions.follow_up: What would you write differently if you were working for that brand?

questions.text: Describe a time you received harsh feedback on your writing. How did you handle it?
questions.good: Describes a specific instance. Shows they listened and made improvements.
questions.great: Shows growth mindset: they sought to understand the feedback, asked clarifying questions, revised the work, and used the experience to improve future writing. Doesn't take it personally.
questions.red_flag: 'I got defensive.' or 'I ignored it because they don't understand writing.' (Can't handle criticism.)
questions.follow_up: What did you learn from that feedback that you still apply today?

questions.text: What projects have you built outside of coursework? Walk me through the most impressive one.
questions.good: Mentions at least one project with a specific tech stack. Explains what it does and why they built it.
questions.great: Project solves a real problem, has users, is hosted live, has a GitHub repo with README. Shows enthusiasm. Can explain technical decisions.
questions.red_flag: No projects outside coursework. Only mentions 'I did a React course.' No portfolio or evidence of work.
questions.follow_up: What was the hardest bug you faced in that project, and how did you fix it?

questions.text: What technologies or tools are you most comfortable with? Rate yourself 1–10 on each.
questions.good: Lists specific tools. Ratings are realistic (not all 10s). Honest about weaknesses.
questions.great: Shows self-awareness. Explains why they rated themselves that way. Mentions what they're actively learning to improve.
questions.red_flag: Rates everything 10/10. Or can't name specific tools. Or 'I don't know any tools yet.'
questions.follow_up: What's the gap between your current skill level and where you want to be?

questions.text: [PRACTICAL EXERCISE] I will share a screen with a simple problem. Solve it out loud.
questions.good: Approaches the problem systematically. Asks clarifying questions. Makes reasonable progress.
questions.great: Breaks the problem down. Explains their thinking process. Not afraid to say 'I don't know' but tries to figure it out. Shows logical thinking.
questions.red_flag: Gives up immediately. Or doesn't ask questions. Or guesses randomly.
questions.follow_up: How would you verify that your solution is correct?

questions.text: How do you learn a new technology or skill? Give me a specific example.
questions.good: Mentions a specific resource (documentation, tutorial, course). Describes a project they built while learning.
questions.great: Shows a structured learning approach: documentation → tutorials → building a project → reading source code → contributing. Describes how they overcame a specific challenge while learning.
questions.red_flag: 'I just watch YouTube videos.' or 'I wait for someone to teach me.' (No self-directed learning.)
questions.follow_up: What do you do when the documentation is confusing or incomplete?

questions.text: Tell me about a time you got stuck on a problem for hours. How did you eventually solve it?
questions.good: Describes a specific problem. Mentions Stack Overflow, documentation, or asking for help.
questions.great: Shows persistence and problem-solving process: broke the problem down, tried different approaches, used debugging tools, asked targeted questions. Describes what they learned from the struggle.
questions.red_flag: 'I give up and ask for help immediately.' or 'I never get stuck because I'm good at coding.' (Either lacks persistence or lacks self-awareness.)
questions.follow_up: What would you do differently if you faced the same problem again?

questions.text: How do you use AI tools (ChatGPT, Copilot, Cursor) in your learning or projects?
questions.good: Mentions using AI for code help, explanations, or debugging. Understands it's a tool, not a replacement for learning.
questions.great: Describes a specific workflow: uses AI to explore concepts, then implements without AI to ensure understanding. Shows critical thinking about AI-generated code. Can explain what the AI-generated code does.
questions.red_flag: 'I don't use AI tools.' or 'I just copy-paste everything from ChatGPT.' (Either resistant or dependent.)
questions.follow_up: Can you explain what this AI-generated code does line by line?

questions.text: What is the difference between AI-generated code and code you write yourself? When should you use each?
questions.good: Understands AI-generated code may have errors or be generic. Knows to review and test it.
questions.great: Discusses the trade-off: AI for boilerplate, exploration, and documentation; human for architecture, business logic, and edge cases. Shows they understand the limitations of AI (hallucinations, bias, security risks).
questions.red_flag: 'AI-generated code is always better.' or 'I never use AI because it's cheating.' (Extreme positions show lack of nuance.)
questions.follow_up: Have you ever found a bug in AI-generated code that you wouldn't have made yourself?

questions.text: Why do you want to intern at Digidelsolutions specifically? What excites you about our work?
questions.good: Mentions the company's focus, products, or mission. Shows they've done basic research.
questions.great: Connects their personal interests (e.g., AI, React, design) to specific projects or technologies the company uses. Shows genuine enthusiasm and a clear idea of what they want to learn.
questions.red_flag: 'I just need an internship.' or 'I don't know much about your company.' (No effort or interest.)
questions.follow_up: What do you hope to have built or learned by the end of your internship?

questions.text: Describe a time you worked in a team. What was your role, and what did you learn about collaboration?
questions.good: Describes a team project. Mentions their role and how they communicated with teammates.
questions.great: Shows they understand team dynamics: they listened to others, resolved conflicts, shared knowledge, and adapted to feedback. Describes a specific challenge they faced as a team and how they overcame it.
questions.red_flag: 'I prefer to work alone.' or 'My teammates slowed me down.' (Not a team player.)
questions.follow_up: What would you do if a team member wasn't pulling their weight?
